literal: the determining intellect; from √budh 'to wake, to know' (cognate with Buddha)

cognates.pie_root: *bʰewdʰ- ‘to be awake, be aware, take heed’
cognates.note: True cognate: Skt. √budh and Greek peúthomai both continue PIE *bʰewdʰ-. The Germanic reflex (Eng. ‘bid/forebode’) developed the sense ‘announce, give notice’ from ‘make aware’; the link is etymological, the meanings have drifted.

invariant_definition: *Buddhi* is the determinative and discriminative function of the inner apparatus: the point at which a merely presented content becomes a judged or settled cognition. In Sāṅkhya it is the first evolute of *prakṛti* and is often identified with *mahat*, the great principle from which more articulated individuality emerges. In practical psychology it is the faculty of *adhyavasāya*, discrimination, resolve, and decision. That makes it central to epistemology and liberation alike. A cognition is not just sensed; it must be taken as this rather than that, true rather than false, to-be-pursued rather than to-be-avoided. Vedānta preserves this role and asks what exactly happens in *buddhi* when liberating knowledge arises. Advaita answers with *vṛtti* and *cidābhāsa*; Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda (Dvaita) treat it as the real instrument through which the self's knowledge functions. Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika uses *buddhi* differently again, as cognition considered as a quality rather than as an inner faculty. The term therefore sits at the junction of ontological psychology and formal epistemology.

school_framing.shared_core: *Buddhi* names **the determinative cognitive function: the *adhyavasāya*-faculty that fixes its object as *idam*-such-and-such**, paradigmatically defined at *Sāṅkhya-Kārikā* 23 (*adhyavasāyo buddhiḥ*) and at *YS* 2.6. All Brāhmaṇical schools accept the structural role. Advaita treats *buddhi* as a *vṛtti*-bearing *upādhi* whose *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti* is the proximate locus of *brahma-jñāna*. Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda treat *buddhi* as a real *prakṛti*-evolute proper to the *jīva*.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Same Φ+E register, same scope-inside. Real disagreement only at the M-level on whether *buddhi* is an *upādhi* or an integral *prakṛti*-evolute of the *jīva* — same pattern as *antaḥkaraṇa* and *ahaṃkāra*.

per_school.definition: *Buddhi* is the locus of the reflected self-consciousness (*ātmaciti-pratibimba* / *cidābhāsa*) by which empirical agency is imputed in relation to meritorious and demeritorious action [[SS I.327](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.327)]. The discipline of *samādhāna* is therefore the steady fixing of *buddhi* in *śuddha-brahman*, not the mere indulgence of mental fluctuation [[VC 26](cite://sankara/vivekacudamani/26)]. When liberating knowledge is already present, what remains is *viśrānti* of that subtle intellect rather than the acquisition of a further object [[JMV, viśrānti citation](cite://vidyaranya/jivanmukti-viveka/citation on *viśrānti*)].

In Yāmuna's formulation, the *ātman* is other than body, senses, *manas*, vital breath, and *dhī*/*buddhi*, and is self-manifest (*ananyasādhana*) rather than disclosed by them [[ĀS 3](cite://yamuna/atma-siddhi/3)]. Cognition therefore cannot be treated as a bodily quality; its occurrence marks the self's distinct mode of manifestation and not the body's own dharma [[ĀS 6](cite://yamuna/atma-siddhi/6)].

*Buddhi* is counted among the real and dependent categories of the created order, alongside *mahat*, *ahaṃkāra*, *manas*, the senses, and the elemental series [[TS 9](cite://madhva/tattva-sankhyana/9)]. Within the Kaṭha hierarchy received by Madhva, *buddhi* stands above *manas* yet below the *ātman*, so it is neither identical with the self nor reducible to sensory traffic [[Kaṭha 1.3.10](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/Upaniṣad 1.3.10)]. Delusive appeal to *upādhi* is traced to *mūḍha-buddhi*, which mistakes an indicator for a producer of difference [[UpK 5](cite://madhva/upadhi-khandana/5)].

In Sāṅkhya, *buddhi* is explicitly glossed as *adhyavasāya*, determinative ascertainment [[KapSS 2.13](cite://kapila/sankhya-sutra/2.13)] [[SK 23](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/23)]. It is the inner instrument that takes in the whole field of objects and thereby makes possible the *puruṣa*'s experience [[SK 35](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/35)] [[SK 37](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/37)]. Its *sāttvika* condition is marked by *dharma*, *jñāna*, *vairāgya*, and *aiśvarya*, so the determinative and discriminative work of *buddhi* is structurally central to Sāṅkhya's path of discernment [[SK 23](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/23)].

In Yoga, *buddhi* is the mutable cognitive principle whose seeming identity with *puruṣa* constitutes *asmitā*, while the sūtra tradition also describes cognition as the self's awareness of its own buddhi-state [[YBh 2.6](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.6)] [[YS 4.22](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.22)]. When *nirvicāra-samādhi* becomes clear, the rajasic and tamasic obscurations of *buddhisattva* fall away and there arises *adhyātmaprasāda* with a lucid *prajñā* [[YBh 1.47](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.47)]. Bhoga and apavarga are accordingly worked out in *buddhi* itself, though they are figuratively predicated of the witnessing *puruṣa* as the enjoyer of their result [[YBh 2.18](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/2.18)].

In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, *buddhi* is not a separate inner organ but a cognition, expressly synonymous with *upalabdhi* and *jñāna* [[NS 1.1.15](cite://gautama/nyaya-sutra/1.1.15)] [[TSaṅg 34](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/34)]. It is counted among the *guṇas*, belongs to the self by *pariśeṣa*, and is non-eternal, perishing through the rise of a subsequent cognition [[TSaṅg 4](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/4)] [[NS 3.2.24](cite://gautama/nyaya-sutra/3.2.24)] [[NS 3.2.39](cite://gautama/nyaya-sutra/3.2.39)]. Later synopsis divides it into *smṛti* and *anubhava*, and then distinguishes *yathārtha* from *ayathārtha* cognition, so the school's *buddhi*-theory is fundamentally a theory of cognitive episodes and their truth-conditions [[TSaṅg 34-35](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/34-35)].

In the early Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā materials on disk, *buddhi* appears chiefly as a cognition-event: Śabara glosses perception as the arising of *buddhi* when sense and object stand in contact [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.4](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.4-pratyaksa-buddhijanma)]. Within that same pramāṇa framework, Vedic *śabda* is said to produce a non-belying cognition of supersensible dharma by virtue of the *autpattika* relation between word and meaning [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.5](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5-sabda-pramana-jñāna)]. The operative use of *buddhi* here is therefore epistemic rather than cosmological: it marks the arising awareness by which objects are apprehended and injunctions become actionable [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.4](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.4-pratyaksa-buddhijanma)] [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.5](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/1.1.5-sabda-pramana-jñāna)].

In Candrakīrti's opening causal sequence, *advayabuddhi* is the non-dual cognition that, together with *mahākaruṇā* and *bodhicitta*, constitutes the bodhisattva's causal formation [[MAv 1.1](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.1)]. The term is therefore soteriological in force: its weight falls on wisdom free from *dvaya*, not on a substantialized faculty-theory of intellect [[MAv 1.1](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/1.1)].

For Dharmakīrti, *buddhi* is an episodic cognition whose operation does not disclose a separately given *sāmānyalakṣaṇa* by *anvaya-vyatireka*; it is always cognition under determinate conditions [[PV 2.5](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.5)]. The verse thus situates *buddhi* inside the pramāṇa-analysis of how cognition functions, rather than treating it as a permanent inner organ standing apart from cognitions [[PV 2.5](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.5)].

In Pratyabhijñā-Trika, what appears as *jñāna*, *saṃkalpa*, *adhyavasāya*, and even *manobuddhi* is a differentiated manifestation of one *citi* under the veiling power of māyā [[ĪPK 1.5.18](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.18)]. Utpaladeva therefore refuses to allow *adhyavasāya* or *buddhi* to stand as an autonomous inert determiner of objects, since what is merely insentient cannot ground the manifestation of an object at all [[ĪPK 1.3.5](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.3.5)]. The doctrinal force of the term lies in this reduction of the determinative function back into reflexive consciousness: *buddhi* is contracted *citi*, not a self-subsistent knower alongside it [[ĪPK 1.5.18](cite://utpaladeva/isvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.18)].

The nearest Jain analogue to *buddhi* is *mati-jñāna*, the first member of the hierarchy of knowledges [[TS 1.9](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9)] [[TS 1.11-12](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.11-12)]. Umāsvāti glosses this *mati* through *smṛti*, *saṃjñā*, *cintā*, and *abhinibodha*, and then analyzes it through *avagraha*, *īhā*, *avāya*, and *dhāraṇā* [[TS 1.13-15](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.13-15)]. The determinative function elsewhere assigned to *buddhi* is thus handled in Jainism as a structured class of cognitions within *mati-jñāna*, not as a distinct ontic organ [[TS 1.13-15](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.13-15)].

textual_history.summary: *Buddhi*, from √budh 'to wake, to be aware', begins in the Ṛgveda as verbal awakening — *bodhi*, 'awake!', *bubodha*, 'he has noticed' — addressed above all to Agni and the gods, with the abstract noun *buddhi* ('the awakened, determining intellect') scarcely yet a fixed term. The Kaṭha Upaniṣad gives the word its first great philosophical image and its place in a hierarchy: in the chariot allegory *buddhi* is the charioteer (*sārathi*) who drives the senses-horses while *manas* serves as the reins (Kaṭha 1.3.3–4), and in the ladder of faculties *buddhi* stands above *manas* and below the 'great self' (1.3.10). Classical Sāṅkhya then makes *buddhi* a cosmological-psychological principle: it is *mahat*, the first evolute of *prakṛti* (SK 22), defined as *adhyavasāya*, determinative ascertainment (SK 23). The *Gītā* takes this determinative *buddhi* and makes it a discipline — *buddhi-yoga*, the yoke of a single resolute intellect (*vyavasāyātmikā buddhi*, 2.41) against the many-branched indecision of the unresolved, with action-in-equanimity defined as *yoga* (2.50). The genealogy is unusually continuous: the Sāṅkhya *mahat/buddhi* and the Gītā's *buddhi-yoga* both presuppose the Kaṭha hierarchy, and the Kaṭha image (charioteer) is the explicit ancestor of the later faculty-psychology.

stages.sanskrit: ātmānaṃ rathinaṃ viddhi śarīraṃ ratham eva tu | buddhiṃ tu sārathiṃ viddhi manaḥ pragraham eva ca || indriyāṇi hayān āhur viṣayāṃs teṣu gocarān
stages.rendering: Know the self as the rider of the chariot, the body as the chariot itself; know the intellect (buddhi) as the charioteer (sārathi), and the mind (manas) as the reins. The senses, they say, are the horses, and the sense-objects their range.
stages.note: The founding image of *buddhi* in Indian thought: the discriminating intellect is the *charioteer* who must master the senses-horses by way of the *manas*-reins. The whole later contrast of *buddhi* (determines, governs) against *manas* (presents, conveys) is anchored here. The verse establishes *buddhi* not as the self but as the self's highest instrument — the faculty of control and direction — directly underwriting the Gītā's call to *buddhi-yoga*.

stages.sanskrit: indriyebhyaḥ parā hy arthā arthebhyaś ca paraṃ manaḥ | manasas tu parā buddhir buddher ātmā mahān paraḥ
stages.rendering: Higher than the senses are their objects; higher than the objects, the mind (manas); higher than the mind, the intellect (buddhi); higher than the intellect, the great self (ātmā mahān).
stages.note: The Kaṭha's faculty-ladder places *buddhi* immediately above *manas* and immediately below the *mahān ātmā*, the 'great self'. This ranking — senses < objects < manas < buddhi < great-self < unmanifest < puruṣa (1.3.11) — is the structural template the Sāṅkhya cosmology and the whole *antaḥkaraṇa* psychology inherit. The phrase *ātmā mahān*, 'great self', is widely read as the prototype of Sāṅkhya's *mahat* = *buddhi*; here, notably, the 'great' is set *above* buddhi, a hierarchy the later systems re-organise.

stages.sanskrit: prakṛter mahāṃs tato 'haṅkāras tasmād gaṇaś ca ṣoḍaśakaḥ | tasmād api ṣoḍaśakāt pañcabhyaḥ pañca bhūtāni
stages.rendering: From prakṛti, the great one (mahat); from that, the I-maker (ahaṅkāra); from that, the set of sixteen; and from five of those sixteen, the five elements.
stages.note: Sāṅkhya makes *buddhi* (under its cosmic name *mahat*, 'the great one') the *first* evolute of unmanifest *prakṛti* — the principle from which I-sense, mind, senses, and elements unfold in series. The Kaṭha's *ātmā mahān* has become *mahat*, the leading product of nature; the term *buddhi* and the term *mahat* are identified by the tradition for this same principle. This is the most ontologically loaded sense of the word: *buddhi* as the architecture of manifested individuality.

stages.sanskrit: adhyavasāyo buddhir dharmo jñānaṃ virāga aiśvaryam | sāttvikam etad rūpaṃ tāmasam asmād viparyastam
stages.rendering: Buddhi is determination (adhyavasāya); its sāttvika form is virtue, knowledge, dispassion, and lordship; its tāmasa form is the reverse of these.
stages.note: The classical definition: *adhyavasāyo buddhiḥ* — *buddhi* is *adhyavasāya*, decisive ascertainment, the function that settles a content as 'this is so, to be done thus'. This is the precise sense the *Gītā* exploits in *vyavasāyātmikā buddhi* (next stage): *vy-ava-so* and *adhi-ava-so* share the root *√so/√si* of resolve. Sāṅkhya thus fixes *buddhi* as the determinative faculty whose *sāttvika* perfection is knowledge and dispassion — the bridge from the Kaṭha charioteer to the Gītā's resolute intellect.

stages.sanskrit: vyavasāyātmikā buddhir ekeha kuru-nandana | bahu-śākhā hy anantāś ca buddhayo 'vyavasāyinām
stages.rendering: Here the resolute intellect (vyavasāyātmikā buddhi) is single, O joy of the Kurus; but many-branched and endless are the intellects of the irresolute.
stages.note: The *Gītā* turns Sāṅkhya's *adhyavasāya*-faculty into a spiritual demand: *vyavasāyātmikā buddhi*, the *buddhi* 'whose essence is resolve', is *one*, set against the infinitely scattered *buddhayaḥ* of those who lack resolution. The determinative faculty of Sāṅkhya (SK 23) becomes the one-pointed will of the practitioner. This is the heart of what the *Gītā* will call *buddhi-yoga* (2.39, 2.49–50), the path of acting through a single steadied intellect.

stages.locus: Bhagavad-Gītā 2.50 (verbatim from Śaṅkara's Gītā-Bhāṣya); cf. 2.39 (buddhi-yoga named)
stages.sanskrit: buddhi-yukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte | tasmād yogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam
stages.rendering: One yoked to [this] intellect (buddhi-yukta) casts off here both good and evil deeds; therefore yoke yourself to yoga — yoga is skill in actions.
stages.note: The culmination of the *buddhi* arc: action performed through the equanimous, resolute *buddhi* (here *buddhi-yukta*) frees one from the binding fruit of deeds, and this is defined as *yoga* — 'skill in actions' (*karmasu kauśalam*). The Kaṭha charioteer who masters the senses, the Sāṅkhya *buddhi* that determines, and the Gītā's resolute intellect that acts without bondage form a single line. Śaṅkara, at 2.39, parses the two named *buddhis* — *sāṅkhya-buddhi* (knowledge of the actionless self) and *yoga-buddhi* (the intellect set on the means) — making the term the explicit hinge between the two paths.

translator_note: 'Intellect' is serviceable but can import specifically modern faculty-psychology assumptions. *Buddhi* is the determinative function or settled cognition-point within a wider inner apparatus, and in Nyāya it can even mean cognition as a quality. Translating every occurrence as 'reason' is especially misleading because much of what *buddhi* does is pre-theoretical determination rather than discursive inference.
